<insights>

- Contains 42% of project files (19/45) — may be too broad, consider splitting
- Generic zone name "Src 2" — enrichment did not assign a meaningful name reflecting this zone's domain purpose
- Contains 40% of project files (19/47) — may be too broad, consider splitting
- The 23 imports from this zone into llm-provider-core confirms this layer acts as a consumer/orchestrator above the core providers — the naming 'src-2' obscures this architectural role.
- Config and project-dirs being co-located with exec and output utilities suggests this zone handles the full CLI surface: reading settings, resolving paths, and rendering results.
- The help-format and output utilities suggest this zone owns the user-facing presentation layer, which is a clean separation from the transport/API concerns in the core zone.
- Cohesion of 0.65 is healthy for a utilities zone; the single entry point (config.ts) provides a clean public surface despite the zone's breadth.
- Auth logic co-located with general utilities rather than in a dedicated security module may make it harder to audit credential handling in isolation.
- 269 internal calls with 0 outgoing (by call graph) and only 4 incoming from core makes this zone nearly self-contained at the call level — the 23 import-level edges to core do not generate proportional call volume, implying they are mostly type or config imports rather than hot-path calls.
- The single-entry-point surface (config.ts) combined with 19 files means the utilities zone has a wide internal surface but a narrow public contract — callers cannot easily reach internal helpers directly.
- llm-cli-utilities exports a single entry point (config.ts) over 19 files, creating a well-controlled public surface that prevents callers from coupling to internal helpers — this is the correct pattern for a utility layer.
- The 23 import edges from llm-cli-utilities into llm-provider-core are unmatched by any test coverage targeting utilities, meaning regressions in how utilities consume core providers would be silent.
- The 269 internal calls with only 4 incoming calls from outside the zone means the utility layer is almost entirely self-serving — the single public entry point (config.ts) hides a densely tangled internal graph that would resist decomposition if the zone ever needs to be split.
- Auth, config, project-dirs, and exec are four distinct concerns currently merged into one zone; each has a different change driver (security policy, user preferences, filesystem layout, subprocess behavior) — co-locating them creates a zone where any concern's change can accidentally break the others.
- auth.ts handles credential management but lives in an undifferentiated utilities zone alongside exec, output, and json helpers — there is no isolation boundary preventing other utilities from importing auth internals, making credential-handling code invisible in security audits of the zone.
- The zone contains 19 files spanning at least four distinct concerns (auth, config, CLI presentation, filesystem/exec) with 269 internal calls indicating deep internal entanglement. A zone this wide with this many internal edges will resist future splitting — the four concern groups should be separated before entanglement grows further.
- help-format.ts is a pure UI presentation utility co-located with credential management (auth.ts) — these have orthogonal change drivers (visual formatting vs security policy) and orthogonal audit scopes, yet they share a zone with no sub-boundary separating them.
- The zone name 'llm-cli-utilities' uses the llm- prefix, but all 19 files inside it (auth, config, exec, output, json, project-dirs) drop this prefix entirely — there is a systematic naming discontinuity between zone identity and file identity that would make a new contributor unsure which zone a new file belongs to.
- Internal call-graph cohesion of 1.0 in a 19-file zone spanning 4 distinct concerns indicates false cohesion — the files are entangled by mutual imports rather than unified by a single concept. High call-graph cohesion in a wide zone is a warning sign of accidental coupling, not intentional design.
- Separate help-format.ts and output.ts (presentation concerns) from auth.ts and exec.ts (security and subprocess concerns) with at minimum a directory-level boundary within the zone. Mixing UI formatting and credential handling in an undifferentiated file set means a security audit of auth.ts must also consider whether output.ts inadvertently logs credentials.
- Establish a file-naming prefix or directory grouping convention that maps visibly to the zone name — e.g. group files under src/config/, src/auth/, src/cli/ subdirectories. The current flat structure with a zone name that does not appear in any filename makes zone membership invisible to contributors and tooling.
- The combination of 42% file concentration, generic name, and being the destination of 23 upward import crossings makes this zone the highest-priority candidate for renaming and internal boundary documentation — not splitting, since global finding 3 confirms four zones is appropriate granularity.
- Manually assign a domain-specific name to this zone (e.g. 'llm-cli-utilities') in the sourcevision configuration. The automated enrichment failure combined with the zone's size means every consumer reading the architecture map gets no signal about what this zone does. A name is zero-cost and eliminates the ambiguity that compounds the architectural risks in global findings 8 and 11.
- [call graph] 269 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
